Predicting Housing PriceÂ¶

Name: Ng Yu Hang
Admin Number: [student-id]
Class: DAAA/FT/1B/07


Project ObjectiveÂ¶

Create a supervised regression model to predict housing prices using the given datas

BackgroundÂ¶

The provided dataset is to predict the housing prices in US based on various information, such as Region, House Size, Renovation status, etc.

ResearchÂ¶

From some quick research, I can determine that factors such as the location, usable space, condition and local market are some of the most important factors contributing to housing prices. We can see if we can feature engineer any of these information out from the information we have from the dataset.

Website reference: https://www.opendoor.com/articles/factors-that-influence-home-value

PhasesÂ¶

  1. Importing data (read csv file)
  2. Data Preparation, Data Exploration and Feature Engineering
  3. Model Preparation and model improvement
  4. Running the models and finding the best model and hyperparameters
  5. Checking if our models run better with the removed features
  6. Comparing our best model to the baseline model
  7. Overfitting reliability check

Importing necessary librariesÂ¶

InÂ [196]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, KFold, GridSearchCV, cross_val_score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression, Ridge, Lasso
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score, mean_absolute_error
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeRegressor
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor, GradientBoostingRegressor, AdaBoostRegressor
from sklearn.svm import SVR
import warnings
from sklearn.dummy import DummyRegressor

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning)

Phase 1: Importing Data (read csv file)Â¶

Importing the csv fileÂ¶

InÂ [197]:
data = pd.read_csv('housing_price_data.csv')
data.head(15)
Out[197]:
House ID City House Area (sqm) No. of Bedrooms No. of Toilets Stories Renovation Status Price ($)
0 0 Chicago 742.0 4 2 3 furnished 1330000
1 1 Denver 896.0 4 4 4 furnished 1225000
2 2 Chicago 996.0 3 2 2 semi-furnished 1225000
3 3 Seattle 750.0 4 2 2 furnished 1221500
4 4 New York 742.0 4 1 2 furnished 1141000
5 5 Boston 750.0 3 3 1 semi-furnished 1085000
6 6 Denver 858.0 4 3 4 semi-furnished 1015000
7 7 New York 1620.0 5 3 2 unfurnished 1015000
8 8 Denver 810.0 4 1 2 furnished 987000
9 9 Seattle 575.0 3 2 4 unfurnished 980000
10 10 Boston 1320.0 3 1 2 furnished 980000
11 11 Boston 600.0 4 3 2 semi-furnished 968100
12 12 Seattle 655.0 4 2 2 semi-furnished 931000
13 13 Boston 350.0 4 2 2 furnished 924000
14 14 Chicago 780.0 3 2 2 semi-furnished 924000

Phase 2: Data Preparation, Data Exploration and Feature EngineeringÂ¶

Selecting Feature columns and separating them into numerical and categoricalÂ¶

InÂ [198]:
categorical_features = ['City', 'Renovation Status']
numerical_features = ['House Area (sqm)', 'No. of Bedrooms', 'No. of Toilets', 'Stories']

We do not need irrelevant columns like House ID. Renovation Status would be changed using Ordinal Encoding below, hence removed.

I will be using ordinal encoding instead of OneHotEncoder for Renovation Status because the values that I assign to it "0", "1", "2" actually signifies improvement. OneHotEncoder is good for Nominal Data, while I am going to use it as Ordinal Data.

Data Exploration: Describing the datasetÂ¶

InÂ [199]:
data.describe().style.background_gradient(cmap="Purples")
Out[199]:
Â  House ID House Area (sqm) No. of Bedrooms No. of Toilets Stories Price ($)
count 545.000000 545.000000 545.000000 545.000000 545.000000 545.000000
mean 272.000000 515.054128 2.965138 1.286239 1.805505 476672.924771
std 157.472220 217.014102 0.738064 0.502470 0.867492 187043.961566
min 0.000000 165.000000 1.000000 1.000000 1.000000 175000.000000
25% 136.000000 360.000000 2.000000 1.000000 1.000000 343000.000000
50% 272.000000 460.000000 3.000000 1.000000 2.000000 434000.000000
75% 408.000000 636.000000 3.000000 2.000000 2.000000 574000.000000
max 544.000000 1620.000000 6.000000 4.000000 4.000000 1330000.000000

Check if there's missing dataÂ¶

InÂ [200]:
data.isnull().sum()
Out[200]:
House ID             0
City                 0
House Area (sqm)     0
No. of Bedrooms      0
No. of Toilets       0
Stories              0
Renovation Status    0
Price ($)            0
dtype: int64

This tells us that this is a fully populated dataset, with no missing data, hence no need for dropping missing rows or imputing any data

General overview of numerical featuresÂ¶

InÂ [201]:
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
for i, column in enumerate(numerical_features):
    plt.subplot(2, 2, i + 1)
    sns.histplot(data[column], kde=True, bins=30)
    plt.title(f'Distribution of {column}')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

From this, we can see tha the majority of the House Area is around 400sqm, with 3 bedrooms, 1 toilet, and 2 stories.

General overview of Categorical FeaturesÂ¶

InÂ [202]:
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 7))
for i, column in enumerate(categorical_features):
    plt.subplot(1, 2, i + 1)
    sns.countplot(
        data=data, 
        x=column, 
        order=data[column].value_counts().index, 
        hue=column,
        legend=True,
        edgecolor=".6"
    )
    plt.title(f'Count of {column}', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
    plt.xlabel(column, fontsize=12)
    plt.ylabel('Count', fontsize=12)
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, fontsize=10)
    plt.yticks(fontsize=10)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.suptitle("Distribution of Categorical Features", fontsize=16, fontweight='bold', y=1.02)
plt.show()

From this, we can see thst most of the houses in this dataset are in New York, and most of them are semi-furnished

Correlation heatmapÂ¶

InÂ [203]:
data_dummies = pd.get_dummies(data, columns=categorical_features, drop_first=True)

correlation_features = numerical_features + [col for col in data_dummies.columns if col not in numerical_features and col != 'Price ($)'] + ['Price ($)']
correlation_matrix = data_dummies[correlation_features].corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, cmap='Purples', fmt='.2f', cbar=True)
plt.title('Correlation Heatmap of Features with Target')
plt.show()

From this, we can see that the House Area has the strongest correlation out of all the other features, with the cities having minimal correlation, and house ID having no correlation tot he house price. Renovation status also have minimal correlation.

Relationship ScatterplotÂ¶

InÂ [204]:
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 10))

for i, column in enumerate(numerical_features):
    plt.subplot(2, 2, i + 1)
    sns.scatterplot(
        data=data, 
        x=column, 
        y='Price ($)', 
        hue='Price ($)', 
        palette='cool', 
        alpha=1, 
        edgecolor=None
    )
    plt.title(f'{column} vs Price ($)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
    plt.xlabel(column, fontsize=12)
    plt.ylabel('Price ($)', fontsize=12)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.suptitle("Relationships Between Numerical Features and Price ($)", fontsize=16, fontweight='bold', y=1.02)
plt.show()

From this, we can tell there is a positive correlation between House Area and Price, where the larger the House Area, the higher the Price. While for No. of Bedrooms vs price, there isnt a very clear trend of increasing price, with houses with 3-4 bedrooms spanning across a wide range of prices.

The number of toilets vs price plot shows that houses with more toilets are generally higher priced, and it seems that most houses have 1 or 2 toilets.

Houses with more stories (3-4) are generally priced higher, and most houses have 2 stories.

Feature EngineeringÂ¶

Finding other useful features by feature engineeringÂ¶

We will create more features and plot them on a correlation heatmap to see how much our target variable is correlated to itÂ¶

From the research we have done, factors that we could get from this dataset could be the condition (aka renovation status), and Total Livable Area. We will feature engineer this, along with some other ratios to increase the model's performance by providing more features so it can try to catch some complex relationship

InÂ [205]:
data['Bedroom_to_Area_Ratio'] = data['No. of Bedrooms'] / data['House Area (sqm)']
data['Toilet_to_Area_Ratio'] = data['No. of Toilets'] / data['House Area (sqm)']
data['Total_Livable_Area'] = data['House Area (sqm)'] * data['Stories']
renovation_map = {'unfurnished': 0, 'semi-furnished': 1, 'furnished': 2}
data['Encoded_Renovation_Status'] = data['Renovation Status'].map(renovation_map)
data['Bedrooms_per_Toilet'] = data['No. of Bedrooms'] / data['No. of Toilets']


correlation_feature_columns = [
    'Bedroom_to_Area_Ratio', 'Toilet_to_Area_Ratio',
    'Total_Livable_Area', 'Encoded_Renovation_Status', 'Bedrooms_per_Toilet'
]

plt.figure(figsize=(14, 10))
newcorrelation_matrix = data[correlation_feature_columns + ['Price ($)']].corr()
sns.heatmap(newcorrelation_matrix, annot=True, cmap='Purples', fmt='.2f', linewidths=0.5)
plt.title('Correlation Heatmap with City-Specific Price per Square Meter Features', fontsize=16)
plt.show()

From the correlation heatmap, we can observe that the Price per squaremeter in each individual cities have little to no correlation to the price of the house, hence we should not include these features to our models.

We can also conclude, that the Total Livable Area has strong correlation to the house price, followed by Encoded Renovation Status. Hence, we will include these into our list of features for our models to learn better.

We also notice that features like Bedroom_to_Area_Ratio has strong correlation with Toilet_to_Area_Ratio, so we can just use one of them to avoid having redundant features.

Final Feature SelectionÂ¶

We make the final selection of features, including our additional feature engineered features which may provide value to training our model.

InÂ [206]:
numerical_features  = numerical_features + [
    'Encoded_Renovation_Status', 'Bedroom_to_Area_Ratio', 
    'Total_Livable_Area', 'Bedrooms_per_Toilet'
]

feature_columns = numerical_features + categorical_features

We have removed the individual city price per sqm, as well as toilet_to_area_ratio to prevent redundancy, and the original Renovation Status since we have it encoded already.

Data PreparationÂ¶

InÂ [207]:
target = 'Price ($)'
y = data[target]
X = data[feature_columns]
X.head()
Out[207]:
House Area (sqm) No. of Bedrooms No. of Toilets Stories Encoded_Renovation_Status Bedroom_to_Area_Ratio Total_Livable_Area Bedrooms_per_Toilet City Renovation Status
0 742.0 4 2 3 2 0.005391 2226.0 2.0 Chicago furnished
1 896.0 4 4 4 2 0.004464 3584.0 1.0 Denver furnished
2 996.0 3 2 2 1 0.003012 1992.0 1.5 Chicago semi-furnished
3 750.0 4 2 2 2 0.005333 1500.0 2.0 Seattle furnished
4 742.0 4 1 2 2 0.005391 1484.0 4.0 New York furnished

Here, we set our y to contain the target data and our X to contain the feature datas.

The above shows the first 5 rows of our X data to see which columns are present in our X dataset

Train-Test Split dataÂ¶

InÂ [208]:
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

print("Training set size:", X_train.shape)
print("Testing set size:", X_test.shape)

X_train = X_train.reset_index(drop=True)
X_test = X_test.reset_index(drop=True)
y_train = y_train.reset_index(drop=True)
y_test = y_test.reset_index(drop=True)

Training set size: (381, 10)
Testing set size: (164, 10)

This shows the number of rows and columns we have in each set.

PreprocessingÂ¶

InÂ [209]:
numerical_preprocessor = Pipeline([
    ('scaler', StandardScaler()) 
])
    
categorical_preprocessor = Pipeline([
    ('onehot', OneHotEncoder(drop='first')) 
])

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ('num', numerical_preprocessor, numerical_features),
    ('cat', categorical_preprocessor, categorical_features)
])

Here, i assign column transformers (StandardScaler and OneHotEncoder) to both dropna data and imputed data.

StandardScaler scales numerical features to have a mean of 0, and a standard deviation of 1. This helps with improving many models' performance.

OneHotEncoder is used on categorical data to create numerical data by creating binary columns.

(drop='first') is used for dropping the first column in the OneHotEncoder binary column to create redundancy (for instance, if there are 3 categories, if 2 columns of the categories are 0, it just means its the 3rd category)

Phase 3: Preparing our ModelsÂ¶

Setting the hyperparameters to tune our modelsÂ¶

InÂ [210]:
param_grids = {
    'Linear Regression': {},

    'Ridge': {
        'ridge__alpha': [0.1, 1.0, 10.0],
        'ridge__solver': ['auto', 'svd', 'saga']
    },

    'Lasso': {
        'lasso__alpha': [0.01, 0.1, 1.0],
        'lasso__max_iter': [5000, 10000, 20000]
    },

    'Decision Tree': {
        'dt__max_depth': [6, 10, None],
        'dt__min_samples_split': [2, 5, 10],
        'dt__min_samples_leaf': [1, 3, 5]
    },

    'Random Forest': {
        'forest__n_estimators': [75, 100, 150],
        'forest__max_depth': [10, 15, None],
        'forest__min_samples_split': [2, 5, 10]
    },

    'Gradient Boosting': {
        'gb__n_estimators': [100, 150, 200],
        'gb__learning_rate': [0.03, 0.05, 0.1],
        'gb__max_depth': [3, 5, 7]
    },

    'AdaBoost': {
        'ab__n_estimators': [75, 100, 150],
        'ab__learning_rate': [0.03, 0.1, 0.2]
    },

    'SVR': {
        'svr__C': [1.0, 10.0, 50.0],
        'svr__epsilon': [0.01, 0.1, 0.5],
        'svr__kernel': ['linear', 'rbf']
    }
}

As the hyperparameters show, for this dataset, I chose a rather balanced approach.

As this is a regression task to predict housing prices, having a model that doesn't overfit too much to outliers and doesn't underfit to the point it couldn't capture patterns is key to making a good model for this task.

Defining our models with pipeline to include our hyperparameter tuning and preprocessorsÂ¶

InÂ [211]:
models = {
    'Linear Regression': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', preprocessor),
        ('linear', LinearRegression())
    ]),
    'Ridge': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', preprocessor),
        ('ridge', Ridge())
    ]),
    'Lasso': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', preprocessor),
        ('lasso', Lasso(alpha=10, max_iter=200000))
    ]),
    'Decision Tree': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', preprocessor),
        ('dt', DecisionTreeRegressor())
    ]),
    'Random Forest': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', preprocessor),
        ('forest', RandomForestRegressor(random_state=42))
    ]),
    'Gradient Boosting': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', preprocessor),
        ('gb', GradientBoostingRegressor(random_state=42))
    ]),
    'AdaBoost': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', preprocessor),
        ('ab', AdaBoostRegressor(random_state=42))
    ]),
    'SVR': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', preprocessor),
        ('svr', SVR())
    ])
}

Using pipeline, I can link the models to use the respective data processing methods using the previously defined preprocessors. Pipelines make the code cleaner and easier to make changes to, as well as make sure the same transformations are consistently applied to both train and test data, preventing Data Leakage.

Setting up our KFoldsÂ¶

InÂ [212]:
kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

Phase 4: Feature Importance and Model Performance TestÂ¶

Feature Importance of models that support itÂ¶

InÂ [213]:
for model_name, pipeline in models.items():
    pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
    best_model = pipeline
    preprocessor = best_model.named_steps.get('preprocessor', None)
    model_step_name = list(best_model.named_steps.keys())[-1]
    model = best_model.named_steps[model_step_name] 

    # Check if the model supports feature_importances_
    if hasattr(model, 'feature_importances_'):
        print(f"Model: {model_name}")
        feature_importances = model.feature_importances_
        feature_names = preprocessor.get_feature_names_out()
        feature_importance_df = pd.DataFrame({
            'Feature': feature_names,
            'Importance': feature_importances
        }).sort_values(by='Importance', ascending=False)

        # Plot feature importances
        plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
        sns.barplot(x='Importance', y='Feature', data=feature_importance_df)
        plt.title(f'{model_name} Feature Importances')
        plt.show()

Model: Decision Tree

Model: Random Forest

Model: Gradient Boosting

Model: AdaBoost

From the feature importance plots, we can see that the City feature and furnished feature doesn't seem very important to the models. Hence we can try removing them to see if we get better results.

Running the modelsÂ¶

InÂ [214]:
results = {}

for model_name, pipeline in models.items():
    print(f"Training {model_name}...")

    grid = GridSearchCV(pipeline, param_grid=param_grids[model_name], cv=kf, scoring='neg_mean_squared_error', verbose=2)
    grid.fit(X_train, y_train)

    best_model = grid.best_estimator_
    y_pred = best_model.predict(X_test)


    mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred)
    mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)
    rmse = np.sqrt(mse)  
    r2 = r2_score(y_test, y_pred)
    mape = np.mean(np.abs((y_test - y_pred) / y_test)) * 100  

    results[model_name] = {
        'Best Parameters': grid.best_params_,
        'MAE': mae,
        'MSE': mse,
        'RMSE': rmse,  
        'RÂ²': r2,
        'MAPE': mape
    }

Training Linear Regression...

Like the partA, i have used the verbose = (number) method to output all the folds to show us that our models are still being trained and the process has not froze

Performance ResultÂ¶

InÂ [215]:
results_df = pd.DataFrame(results).T

results_df = results_df.style.apply(
    lambda x: [
        "background-color: green; color: white" if v == x.min() else 
        "background-color: red; color: white" if v == x.max() else "" 
        for v in x
    ],
    subset=['MAE', 'MSE', 'MAPE', 'RMSE']
).apply(
    lambda x: [
        "background-color: green; color: white" if v == x.max() else 
        "background-color: red; color: white" if v == x.min() else "" 
        for v in x
    ],
    subset=['RÂ²']
)

print("Model Evaluation Results:\n")
print("Legend: \nGreen - Best Performance; Red - Worst Performance")
results_df

Model Evaluation Results:

Legend: 
Green - Best Performance; Red - Worst Performance
Out[215]:
Â  Best Parameters MAE MSE RMSE RÂ² MAPE

Random Forest {'forest__max_depth': 10, 'forest__min_samples_split': 10, 'forest__n_estimators': 100} 106367.971448 21352094295.873825 146123.558319 0.504177 24.802835
Gradient Boosting {'gb__learning_rate': 0.03, 'gb__max_depth': 3, 'gb__n_estimators': 150} 107659.263578 21273643419.498463 145854.871086 0.505998 25.398451
AdaBoost {'ab__learning_rate': 0.03, 'ab__n_estimators': 150} 114049.023620 22634827830.904278 150448.754833 0.474390 27.638326
SVR {'svr__C': 50.0, 'svr__epsilon': 0.01, 'svr__kernel': 'linear'} 141807.217940 35706922472.160217 188962.754193 0.170839 32.447890

From this evaluation result, we can see that our top 3 best models are: Ridge Regression, followed by Linear Regression and Random Forest. Despite having no hyperparameter tuning, Linear Regression is performing well on this problem, which can be due to reasons like: the data is clean, and has no significant outliers. Linear Regression also assumes a linear relationship, hence, if it performs well, it means it'd likely be a linear relationship

In conclusion from this evaluation, Ridge Regression is our best model, and SVR is our worst model

Selecting the best models, parameters and trying less features for this taskÂ¶

feature_columns

InÂ [217]:
columns_to_drop = ['City', 'Renovation Status', 'Stories']

# Update feature_columns by removing the specified columns
newfeature_columns = [feature for feature in feature_columns if feature not in columns_to_drop]
newfeature_columns

Removing the unnecessary features from what the feature importance showedÂ¶

InÂ [218]:
tuned_X = data[newfeature_columns]

tuned_X_train, tuned_X_test, tuned_y_train, tuned_y_test = train_test_split(tuned_X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

print("Training set size:", X_train.shape)
print("Testing set size:", X_test.shape)

tuned_X_train = tuned_X_train.reset_index(drop=True)
tuned_X_test = tuned_X_test.reset_index(drop=True)
tuned_y_train = tuned_y_train.reset_index(drop=True)
tuned_y_test = tuned_y_test.reset_index(drop=True)

Training set size: (381, 10)
Testing set size: (164, 10)

Defining our new models with the best parametersÂ¶

InÂ [219]:
newpreprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ('num', numerical_preprocessor, newfeature_columns)
])

There are no more categorical features after we removed cities, so we can just use the feature columns for preprocessing

InÂ [238]:
newparam_grids = {
    'Linear Regression': {},  
    'Ridge': {
        'ridge__alpha': 10, 
        'ridge__solver': 'auto'
    },
    'Random Forest': {
        'forest__max_depth': [10], 
        'forest__min_samples_split': [10], 
        'forest__n_estimators': [100]
    }
}

We now use the best hyperparameters as stated previously from the evaluation results, so we can re-run the best model faster.

InÂ [239]:
best_models = {
    'Linear Regression': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', newpreprocessor),
        ('linear', LinearRegression())
    ]),
    'Ridge': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', newpreprocessor),
        ('ridge', Ridge())
    ]),
    'Random Forest': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', newpreprocessor),
        ('forest', RandomForestRegressor(random_state=42))
    ]),
}

Pipelining our newpreprocessor with the best models only

Our new feature importances for the rest of the featuresÂ¶

InÂ [240]:
for model_name, pipeline in best_models.items():
    best_model = pipeline
    preprocessor = best_model.named_steps['preprocessor']
    model_step_name = list(best_model.named_steps.keys())[-1]
    model = best_model.named_steps[model_step_name]

    if hasattr(model, 'feature_importances_'):
        feature_importances = model.feature_importances_
        feature_names = preprocessor.get_feature_names_out()
        feature_importance_df = pd.DataFrame({
            'Feature': feature_names,
            'Importance': feature_importances
        }).sort_values(by='Importance', ascending=True)

        plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
        sns.barplot(x='Importance', y='Feature', data=feature_importance_df)
        plt.title(f'{model_name} Feature Importances')
        plt.xlabel("Feature Importance")
        plt.ylabel("Features")
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

We are now left with data that were somewhat useful to very useful to most of the models with feature importance.

Phase 5: Checking if our models run better with the removed featuresÂ¶

Training our models with the best hyperparametersÂ¶

InÂ [241]:
tuned_results = {}

for model_name, pipeline in best_models.items():
    print(f"\nTraining and evaluating model: {model_name}\n")
    
    cv_scores = cross_val_score(
        pipeline, 
        tuned_X_train, 
        tuned_y_train, 
        cv=kf, 
        scoring='neg_mean_squared_error', 
        verbose=3
    )

    pipeline.fit(tuned_X_train, tuned_y_train)
    tuned_y_pred = pipeline.predict(tuned_X_test)

    mae = mean_absolute_error(tuned_y_test, tuned_y_pred)
    mse = mean_squared_error(tuned_y_test, tuned_y_pred)
    rmse = np.sqrt(mse)
    r2 = r2_score(tuned_y_test, tuned_y_pred)
    mape = np.mean(np.abs((tuned_y_test - tuned_y_pred) / tuned_y_test)) * 100

    tuned_results[model_name] = {
        'MAE': mae,
        'MSE': mse,
        'RMSE': rmse,
        'RÂ²': r2,
        'MAPE': mape
    }

Display Results of our tuned modelsÂ¶

InÂ [242]:
tuned_results_df = pd.DataFrame(tuned_results).T

tuned_results_df = tuned_results_df.style.apply(
    lambda x: [
        "background-color: green; color: white" if v == x.min() else
        "background-color: red; color: white" if v == x.max() else "" 
        for v in x
    ],
    subset=['MAE', 'MSE', 'MAPE', 'RMSE']
).apply(
    lambda x: [
        "background-color: green; color: white" if v == x.max() else
        "background-color: red; color: white" if v == x.min() else "" 
        for v in x
    ],
    subset=['RÂ²']
)

print("Model Evaluation Results:\n")
print("Legend: \nGreen - Best Performance; Red - Worst Performance")
tuned_results_df

Green - Best Performance; Red - Worst Performance

Ridge 106447.500791 20525450480.007473 143267.059996 0.523372 24.991854

From this, we can tell that Ridge still remains the best model, outperforming every other models. Hence, Ridge is the most suitable model for this task.

Full Feature VS Removed FeatureÂ¶

InÂ [243]:
results_df = pd.DataFrame(results).T
results_df = results_df.drop(columns=["Best Parameters"], errors='ignore')
tuned_results_df = pd.DataFrame(tuned_results).T


models_to_compare = tuned_results_df.index
comparison_df = results_df.loc[models_to_compare] - tuned_results_df


comparison_df = comparison_df.style.apply(
    lambda x: [
        "background-color: green; color: white" if v > 0 else 
        "background-color: red; color: white" 
        for v in x
    ],
    subset=['MAE', 'MSE', 'MAPE', 'RMSE']
).apply(
    lambda x: [
        "background-color: green; color: white" if v < 0 else 
        "background-color: red; color: white" 
        for v in x
    ],
    subset=['RÂ²']
)

print("Green = improvement, red = decreased performance")

comparison_df

Green = improvement, red = decreased performance
Out[243]:
Â  MAE MSE RMSE RÂ² MAPE
Linear Regression 983.516572 239962144.218609 834.285489 -0.005572 0.063958
Ridge 299.803022 -35746241.449337 -124.808238 0.000830 -0.096592
Random Forest -3091.590158 -1261558606.777611 -4254.807487 0.029295 -0.507088

BUT, by further inspection, we can see that the performance for this set of models dropped, compared to the ones with the Renovation Status, cities and num of stories features. Hence our check for whether removing the features would help with performance proved to be unsuccessful

Phase 6: Comparing our best model with best features to a baseline dummy modelÂ¶

Since the models have failed to show that the removed features help with performance, we will be using back the previously untouched features that the first set of models trained on, with the best hyperparameters to be used to conclude this part of the assignment.

We will now use a scatter plot and compare how our best model compares to a dummy baseline model.

InÂ [244]:
# Fit a Dummy Regressor
dummy_model = DummyRegressor(strategy="mean")
dummy_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
dummy_predictions = dummy_model.predict(X_test)

# Final model predictions
final_model = best_models['Ridge']  
final_predictions = final_model.predict(X_test)

# Plot Actual vs Predicted Trends
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))

plt.scatter(y_test, final_predictions, label="Final Model (Ridge)", alpha=0.7, color='green')
plt.scatter(y_test, dummy_predictions, label="Baseline Model (Dummy Regressor)", alpha=0.7, color='blue')
plt.plot([min(y_test), max(y_test)], [min(y_test), max(y_test)], 
         color='red', linestyle='--', label="Ideal Fit")

plt.title("Actual vs Predicted Trends: Final Model vs Dummy Regressor")
plt.xlabel("Actual Values")
plt.ylabel("Predicted Values")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

From this, we can see that our final model, Ridge has more plots closer to the ideal fit line, compared to our baseline model, hence showing that our final model is better than the baseline dummy model

InÂ [245]:
mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, dummy_predictions)
mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, dummy_predictions)
rmse = np.sqrt(mse)
r2 = r2_score(y_test, dummy_predictions)
mape = np.mean(np.abs((y_test - dummy_predictions) / y_test)) * 100

final_mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, final_predictions)
final_mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, final_predictions)
final_rmse = np.sqrt(final_mse)
final_r2 = r2_score(y_test, final_predictions)
final_mape = np.mean(np.abs((y_test - final_predictions) / y_test)) * 100

dummy_results = {
    'MAE': mae,
    'MSE': mse,
    'RMSE': rmse,
    'RÂ²': r2,
    'MAPE': mape,
}

final_results = {
    'MAE': final_mae,
    'MSE': final_mse,
    'RMSE': final_rmse,
    'RÂ²': final_r2,
    'MAPE': final_mape,
}

dummy_results_df = pd.DataFrame(dummy_results, index=["Dummy Model"]).T
dummy_results_df.columns = ["Dummy Model"]

combined_results = pd.DataFrame({
    "Dummy Model": dummy_results,
    "Final Model (Ridge)": final_results
}).T

combined_results_df = combined_results.style.apply(
    lambda x: [
        "background-color: green; color: white" if v == x.min() else
        "background-color: red; color: white" if v == x.max() else "" 
        for v in x
    ],
    subset=['MAE', 'MSE', 'RMSE', 'MAPE']
).apply(
    lambda x: [
        "background-color: green; color: white" if v == x.max() else
        "background-color: red; color: white" if v == x.min() else "" 
        for v in x
    ],
    subset=['RÂ²']
)
combined_results_df
Out[245]:
Â  MAE MSE RMSE RÂ² MAPE
Dummy Model 161771.174173 43065975934.607613 207523.434664 -0.000048 39.536136
Final Model (Ridge) 106447.500791 20525450480.007473 143267.059996 0.523372 24.991854

To better view our model's superior performance comapred to the Dummy model, I have created a table to better visualize and compare their differences.

Final Phase: Overfitting Reliability CheckÂ¶

As a final check before we conclude the assignment, we want to make sure our model is actually reliable, and not overfitting, giving a misleading result. Hence we will compare the difference between the Train R2 score vs Test R2 Score to determine if they're overfitting.

InÂ [251]:
ridge_model = models['Ridge']
best_params = newparam_grids['Ridge']

ridge_model.set_params(**best_params)
ridge_model.fit(X_train, y_train)


train_predictions = ridge_model.predict(X_train)
test_predictions = ridge_model.predict(X_test)
train_r2 = r2_score(y_train, train_predictions)
test_r2 = r2_score(y_test, test_predictions)

train_test_results = {
    'Train RÂ²': train_r2,
    'Test RÂ²': test_r2
}
overfitting = train_r2 - test_r2

print(f"Model: Ridge")
print(f"Train RÂ²: {train_r2:.4f}")
print(f"Test RÂ²: {test_r2:.4f}\n")
print(f"Overfitting test (Train RÂ² - Test RÂ²): {overfitting:.4f}\n")

if overfitting > 0.1:
    print("Conclusion: This model might be overfitting.")
elif train_r2 < 0.3 and test_r2 < 0.3:
    print("Conclusion: This model might be underfitting.")
else:
    print("Conclusion: No overfitting or underfitting detected. The model generalizes well.")

Model: Ridge
Train RÂ²: 0.5960
Test RÂ²: 0.5242

Overfitting test (Train RÂ² - Test RÂ²): 0.0718

Conclusion: No overfitting or underfitting detected. The model generalizes well.

From this test, we can see that our model is not overfitting, hence the result of it being the best performing model is reliable

ConclusionÂ¶

From the above testing, we can conclude that Ridge Regression is the best performing model suitable for this task.

We can also conclude that the features we should train our data with are:
['House Area (sqm)', 'No. of Bedrooms', 'No. of Toilets', 'Stories', 'Encoded_Renovation_Status', 'Bedroom_to_Area_Ratio', 'Total_Livable_Area', 'Bedrooms_per_Toilet', 'City', 'Renovation Status']

QNAÂ¶

âœ”ï¸ How is your prediction task defined? And what is the meaning of the output variable?


âžœ The task is a regression task to predict the house price (Price ($)) based on the features provided in the dataset as factors that it depends on.
âžœ The output variable is `Price ($)`, which represents the price of the house in U.S. Dollars

âœ”ï¸Did you process the features in any way?


âžœ Yes, since this has no missing datas, I did not have to impute any data or drop any rows, hence I only used StandardScaler() for numerical features and OneHotEncoder for the categorical features for better model performance, and allow the models to be trained with them effectively.

For this task, i have also feature engineered new features to help the models catch more complex relationships between the features to perform better.

âœ”ï¸ How did you select which learning algorithms to use?


âžœ Since this is a regression problem, I have selected models that are made for regression problems. Also specifically adding Lasso and Ridge, as these 2 models have Regularization Penalties. It helps with reducing overfitting, as well as generalizing over noisy data. Noise datas are datas that are datas in the dataset that are irrelevant that can disrupt the pattern.

âœ”ï¸ Did you try to tune the hyperparameters of the learning algorithm, and how?


âžœ Yes, I used hyperparameters to set the complexity of the learning algorithms. Since this dataset has no extremely obvious class imbalances, I will be setting the hyperparameters to be fairly balanced in terms of underfitting and overfitting.

âœ”ï¸ How do you evaluate the quality of your machine learning models?


âžœ I run my models using my hyperparameters and features, by using GridSearchCV, it takes the best performing hyperparameter model and show me their MAE, RMSE, MSE, R^2, MAPE For Regression models, I generally look more towards the MAE, MSE, R^2, MAPE metrics. From there, using the feature importances plots, I test for performance changes after removing unimportant features, to which they showed a drop in performance as seen in their MAE, RSME, MSE, R^2 and MAPE score afterwards.

âœ”ï¸ How well does your system compare to a dummy baseline?


âžœ According to my scatterplot of Actual vs Predicted Trends, as well as my evaluation results, to which most of my models have better MAE, MSE, R^2, MAPE scores than the baseline dummy model. However, the dummy baseline assumes a constant and simplistic pattern and does not try to learn the pattern, hence any properly made model should be able to outperform this.

âœ”ï¸ Is it possible to say something about which features the model considers important? (Whether this is possible depends on the type of classifier you are using)


âžœ Yes, under Phase 4, I have set up bar graphs to show the importance of each features that the models find important. I have then subsequently tested for better model performance by removing unimportant features, to which the model failed to show any improvement.
